A senior reviewer scanning the added lines should find no residue from the writing process — no commented-out code, no leftover debug/trace output, no throwaway scaffolding shipped as if final — and any comment that IS present should explain the WHY (intent, a non-obvious constraint, a unit, a caveat, a reference) rather than merely restate the WHAT the very next line already says.

GOOD vs BAD (open, illustrative — reason about cases not listed):
- BAD, e.g. an added line that is itself plausible source behind a comment marker (a commented-out assignment, call, or control statement); a bare trace print or breakpoint left in shipped code; a "TEMP/remove-before-merge" stub; a comment like "// increment i" above i++ that adds nothing.
- GOOD/clean, e.g. a comment giving a reason ("retry once: upstream drops the first call after idle"); output that is the file's deliberate purpose (a CLI's own stdout, a test's expected-output); a logger call through a real logging facility; a sanctioned TODO referencing a tracked issue. When unsure whether a comment restates or explains natural-language intent, treat it as explaining and do NOT flag — but that benefit of the doubt applies ONLY to prose. Commented-out source is never an explanatory comment: if the text after the comment marker would itself parse as code in this file's language (it opens with a control/declaration keyword such as `case`/`if`/`guard`/`for`/`while`/`return`/`func`/`let`/`var`, or is an assignment or a `name(...)` call — especially when an identical live statement appears elsewhere in the diff), it is commented-out code (PRESENT, BAD), not a WHY comment.

GROUNDING & CONTEXT BOUNDARY: judge ONLY lines the diff marks as ADDED (leading '+'); removed and unchanged lines are not graded — code the author commented out and DELETED is a cleanup, never a blemish. Quote the exact [L<n>] span and file path for every blemish; a blemish you cannot quote is not counted. Never infer that a line is dead or debug-only unless its quoted text shows it. Exclude generated/vendored files (lockfiles, *.gen.*, specs, build output, migrations) — if no hand-written added surface remains, or the diff is absent/empty, emit NOT_APPLICABLE.

SEVERITY (principle, not a counter): this is cosmetic residue with no functional impact, so it is a gentle, NON-BLOCKING nudge — never a merge gate, and never exceeds MAJOR on this practice alone. A lone stray comment or marker is the lightest touch; a scatter of leftovers is a modest cleanup; reserve the top of the range for residue with real bite — e.g. a debug print left inside an otherwise-empty catch, where swallowed handling crosses into functional risk. Sheer line count does not raise severity: a long commented-out block is still one cleanliness nudge. Identical added lines must yield identical severity.

DEFER: this practice judges only residue-on-added-lines and WHAT-vs-WHY comments. It does NOT judge naming, function/file size, scope, duplication, dead branches in live code, swallowed errors, or runtime defects — sibling practices own those; do not borrow their complaint.

STANCE: describe the observable behaviour — what the author left behind in the code — never impute intent or character. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most consequential leftover (the block to delete, the print to drop, the one WHAT comment to turn into a WHY) and show the shape of the fix; never rewrite the whole change.
practices.whyItMatters: Stray debug output, blocks of commented-out code, and comments that just echo the line all add noise the next reader has to wade through. Clean code with a comment that explains the why reads like someone meant it.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: No leftover print statements or commented-out branches, and where a line looks surprising, a short comment gives the reason, such as 'retry once: the upstream API drops the first call after an idle period', rather than restating what the line does.

areas.name: Handling failure well
areas.description: Fail safely when things go wrong. Handle errors instead of swallowing them, check inputs and edge cases where data enters, and do not crash on a problem the code could recover from.

practices.name: Handle errors instead of swallowing them
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any "assessment=GOOD" rule below): this practice catches a specific defect, so the ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the diff adds failure-handling constructs but none swallow a failure. A GOOD endorsement here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes exist: PRESENT+BAD (a quotable swallowed failure) or NOT_APPLICABLE.

STANDARD: When this change introduces a failure path — a call that can throw, reject, or return an error — the failure should stay visible: surfaced to a caller who can react, or recorded with enough context that someone can diagnose it later. The defect this practice names is a failure that is caught or received and then SILENTLY DROPPED, so downstream code carries on with bad or missing data and no one learns it failed. Judge this as a craft habit in how the ADDED code handles errors, not a line-by-line lint sweep.

GOOD (→ NOT_APPLICABLE, the baseline): the error propagates (rethrows, wraps with context, returns/forwards it), is recorded meaningfully (logged at a level a human would notice, a failure metric, an error state the UI reads), or is deliberately absorbed with an in-diff comment naming WHY that is safe. BAD (→ PRESENT): the failure is discarded — e.g. an empty or no-op catch, a caught error variable that is never used, an ignored error return, a dropped async rejection. These are illustrations, not a closed list; recognise the same shape in any language or idiom using your judgment as an experienced reviewer.

SEVERITY (non-blocking, formative — a habit to grow, never a merge gate; never exceeds MAJOR): a swallow on a best-effort or clearly secondary path (cleanup, teardown, optional telemetry, or merely logged a touch too quietly) is MINOR; a swallow that hides a real fault from the caller or user — on a path that persists, communicates, authenticates, parses, or otherwise carries consequence — is MAJOR. Multiple distinct swallows lean MAJOR. Identical constructs must yield identical severity. Name the single most consequential swallowed failure, not an exhaustive list.

PROCESS STANCE: before concluding NOT_APPLICABLE, actually scan the added hunks for failure paths and check where each error goes — don't assume a clean surface from a glance.

GROUNDING (mandatory): judge only constructs this diff ADDS or MODIFIES (new-side [L<n>] markers); never grade pre-existing context or assume code you cannot see. Per-commit patches are not in your context — reason from what the diff shows. Every PRESENT finding must quote the exact added hunk that shows the discard; if you cannot point to the dropped error in a quoted added line, you do not have a finding. If the diff is absent/unenriched, or the hand-written change adds no failure path at all, emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never invent a construct, an exoneration, or a clean bill you did not verify. Describe the observable behaviour; never impute intent or character.

DEFER: this practice does not judge whether inputs/edge cases are validated (validates-inputs-and-edge-cases-at-the-boundary), nor a deliberate fail-fast crash on an unrecoverable state (avoids-unsafe-panics-and-chosen-crashes) — those are sibling practices.

Guidance is feed-forward: show the shape of surfacing that one error (rethrow with context, log it where it'll be seen, return it, or comment why it's safely absorbed) — never rewrite the whole change.
practices.whyItMatters: An empty catch block turns a failure into a silent one: the code carries on as if nothing went wrong, and you find out only when something downstream breaks with no clue why. Surfacing the error saves the 2am debugging session.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A failed call logs what went wrong and passes the failure up to a caller who can react, instead of catching the exception and quietly continuing with bad or missing data.

practices.name: Validate inputs and edge cases at the boundary
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE: this practice exists to catch one specific defect, so a clean surface is NOT_APPLICABLE, never (PRESENT, GOOD) — the absence of the defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse, and a GOOD here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot execute or verify. Only two outcomes exist: (PRESENT, BAD) — a quotable instance of the defect — or NOT_APPLICABLE.

THE STANDARD. Judge whether added or modified code that consumes a value it does not control handles the obvious failure case on purpose, instead of assuming a happy path. A boundary value is one entering from outside the local logic — a request param/body/header, a parsed string or number, a value fetched by key or index from a collection, a file/stream/env/config read, the result of a third-party or RPC call. A site is in scope only when the same change also USES that value: dereferences it, indexes it, computes on it, or passes it into logic that assumes a shape. A value merely forwarded untouched, or one whose shape its type already guarantees, is not a boundary-consuming site. This is the defensive-programming habit at module boundaries (McConnell; SEI CERT input-validation): does the obvious failure case meet a guard, or a crash three layers deep?

GOOD (→ NOT_APPLICABLE, abstain) looks like: each introduced consuming site handles the obvious bad case — a null/empty/missing-key check, a try/catch or error-return around a parse that can fail, a bounds check before indexing, a range/sign/finite check before arithmetic, a default for an absent config, or a framework guard visible in the same hunk (e.g. a Bean Validation annotation, an early return on the bad case). Judge the HABIT — is the obvious failure handled at all — not an exhaustive lint of every conceivable input. BAD looks like: a site reads an uncontrolled value and then dereferences/indexes/computes on it with no guard in the same hunk or its immediate visible context, so a malformed or absent input breaks the new path.

SEVERITY (non-blocking, formative). A single unguarded site is MINOR; several unguarded introduced sites is MAJOR. Never exceed MAJOR on this practice alone — it is a habit to grow, not a merge gate. Describe the observable code behaviour; never impute intent or character. Identical hunks must yield identical severity; use your judgment as an experienced reviewer for everything in between.

GROUNDING / ANTI-FABRICATION. Judge only the added/modified lines and their visible in-diff context ([L<n>] new-side markers); per-commit and surrounding code outside the diff are not in your context — never claim to run, compile, or test it. Before concluding, actually scan the changed lines for an uncontrolled value that gets consumed — look before you leap. A (PRESENT, BAD) finding REQUIRES quoting the exact [L<n>] span of each unguarded site and naming the missing guard (e.g. "null check before .name", "catch around the parse"). Never assert a missing guard you cannot point to: if a guard could plausibly sit just above the visible context, treat the site as guarded. If the diff is absent/empty, consumes no boundary input, or only generated/vendored files remain, emit NOT_APPLICABLE — never invent an input surface or a clean bill of health.

DEFER. This judges robustness against malformed/absent/out-of-range input only — not whether untrusted input reaches a dangerous sink unescaped (a sibling security practice), and not the quality of error handling once input is guarded (a sibling error practice; a catch that merely prevents the crash satisfies the guard here regardless of its body). When a hunk's only defect belongs to a sibling, abstain rather than co-flag.
practices.whyItMatters: Data from a user, a file, or another service will not always arrive in the shape you expected: it can be empty, null, negative, or huge. Checking it where it enters means a clear rejection instead of a confusing crash three layers deep.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: Code that reads an external value first asks what happens if it is missing or out of range and handles that on purpose, so an empty list or a malformed payload meets a clean guard instead of a null-pointer surprise.

practices.name: Avoid crashing on recoverable problems
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any "assessment=GOOD" rule below): this practice catches one specific defect, so the ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the diff adds the relevant constructs but none exhibit the defect. Endorsing GOOD here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot verify. Only two outcomes exist: (PRESENT, BAD) with a quoted instance, or NOT_APPLICABLE.

STANDARD. Code should degrade gracefully on ordinary, recoverable runtime failure rather than deliberately aborting. A "chosen crash" is an added construct that hard-stops the process on a condition an ordinary program could survive — force-unwrapping/force-casting a value that can legitimately be absent, or a deliberate abort/panic/exit/fatal-error — when that value rides an ordinary runtime path (parsed input, network/DB/disk/env, decoded payload, a fallible API's result). Judge whether the failure was recoverable and whether the author chose to crash on it anyway. Use your judgment as an experienced reviewer across languages and stacks; do not lean on a fixed list of keywords.

GOOD vs BAD (open examples, not a checklist). Bad, e.g.: force-unwrapping a value decoded from JSON or fetched from a server; panicking on a fallible parse; calling a fatal abort on a path that could return an error instead. Acceptable — not a chosen crash — e.g.: a genuine programmer invariant that cannot hold unless surrounding code is already broken (an unreachable enum default, a precondition on a value just proven non-nil); a fail-fast at a CLI/startup entry point where aborting IS the contract; an operation that is total by language contract for its operand type and provably cannot fail; force-unwrapping an author-controlled literal/constant. Reason about totality and provenance, not the spelling of the construct.

PROCESS STANCE. Before concluding NOT_APPLICABLE, actually scan the added lines for crash-shaped constructs and trace where each crashed-on value came from — look before you leap. A crash you cannot tie to a runtime-sourced value is not groundable.

PRESENCE / ASSESSMENT / SEVERITY (principles, not a lookup table). PRESENT, BAD only when you can quote the deciding added line and name the runtime source of the value it crashes on. Decide by whether the operation can ACTUALLY return nil/throw on some real input, not by the mere presence of `!`/force-unwrap: a conversion that is total by language contract (e.g. Swift `String.data(using: .utf8)` for a Unicode-capable encoding, or indexing a literal-bounded array) provably cannot fail and is NOT_APPLICABLE here, never a finding. Severity then scales with how clearly the failure was recoverable and how ordinary the source is: a clear recoverable runtime source warrants the ceiling; genuine uncertainty about recoverability lowers it — and when you cannot demonstrate a concrete input on which the unwrapped operation yields nil, do not exceed MINOR. This finding is non-blocking and NEVER exceeds MAJOR on its own. Identical hunks must yield identical severity.

GROUNDING + ANTI-FABRICATION. Cite the exact added-line span and the value's origin from observable diff evidence. Never invent a file, line, value source, or clean bill you did not verify. If the relevant fact is not observable, return NOT_APPLICABLE. Judge only added/modified lines (each carries an [L<n>] new-side marker); never reason about removed lines or code outside the diff, and never claim to compile or run it. CONTEXT BOUNDARY: you see the diff, not the whole program — if you cannot see enough to know a value is runtime-sourced and recoverable, abstain rather than guess. Docs/config/data-only or generated/vendored-only diffs carry no surface — NOT_APPLICABLE.

NON-BLOCKING / NO INTENT. Frame this as a defensive-programming habit to grow, describing the observable behaviour of the code; never impute intent or character.

DEFER. Empty/swallowed catches, logged-and-continued errors, and missing input validation belong to sibling practices — judge only the deliberate-crash construct here.

FEED-FORWARD. When BAD, name the single most consequential chosen crash and show the shape of the graceful alternative for that line (guard/if-let, try-with-fallback, return-an-error, Result); do not rewrite the whole change.
practices.whyItMatters: Reaching for a force-unwrap or a panic on a condition that can actually happen turns an ordinary hiccup into a hard crash for your users. A problem the code can recover from deserves a graceful path, not a deliberate stop.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: Where a value might legitimately be absent, the code unwraps it safely and falls back to a default or a clear error, instead of force-unwrapping and bringing the whole thing down.

areas.name: Making changes secure by default
areas.description: Make each change secure by default. Validate and escape untrusted input before it reaches a dangerous sink, avoid insecure defaults and over-broad permissions, and change dependencies deliberately.

practices.name: Neutralize untrusted input before a dangerous sink
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any "assessment=GOOD" rule below): this practice exists to catch a specific defect. The ABSENCE of that defect is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse — emit NOT_APPLICABLE, never assessment=GOOD, when the diff carries untrusted input toward a dangerous sink but the flow is neutralized. A GOOD endorsement here would be a clean bill of health on logic whose correctness you cannot fully verify, which is exactly what you must not give. Only two outcomes exist: (PRESENT, BAD) for a quotable instance of the defect, or NOT_APPLICABLE.

THE STANDARD. When code added in this diff carries a value from outside the trust boundary into an operation where that value can change the operation's meaning, the value must be neutralized first — parameterized, escaped for the exact sink context, or validated against an allow-list/strict type — rather than concatenated or interpolated raw. Reason as an experienced security reviewer: a "source" is any externally-controlled value (e.g. a request param, header, body, uploaded filename, CLI arg, or a remote service's response); a "dangerous sink" is any place where raw text changes execution (e.g. a built query string, a shell command, an HTML/template render, a filesystem path, a redirect target, a deserializer/eval, or a constructed URL whose path/query interpolates the value — e.g. `"/auth/\(token)"` then `URL(string: base + path)`, where the fix is percent-encoding via the platform URL builder, not raw interpolation). These lists are illustrative, not exhaustive — recognize the shape in any language or stack, including sinks not named here.

GOOD looks like: the untrusted value reaches the sink bound as a parameter, run through a context-correct encoder, passed as an args array to exec, confined under a canonicalized base path, or rendered through auto-escaping left on. BAD looks like: the value is string-concatenated/interpolated straight into the sink with nothing in the quoted hunk neutralizing it.

PROCESS STANCE (look before you leap). Before concluding NOT_APPLICABLE, actually scan the added lines for a source-to-sink flow — don't skip it because the diff "looks routine." But trace only what you can see: judge added/modified lines and their immediate in-diff context, and never assume code outside the diff sanitizes (or fails to sanitize) the value.

GROUNDING & ANTI-FABRICATION. A (PRESENT, BAD) finding must quote the exact added [L<n>] hunk where the source reaches the sink unneutralized, and name both the source and the sink end-to-end. If you cannot point to both on quoted added lines, it is not assertable — return NOT_APPLICABLE. Never claim the code runs, compiles, is exploitable, or that a payload triggers; assert only the visible habit. Never invent a file, line, or clean bill you did not verify. If the diff is dominated by generated/vendored files, assess only the hand-written remainder.

SEVERITY (philosophy, not a lookup). This is a non-blocking, formative signal — a security habit to grow, never a merge gate, and it never exceeds MAJOR on this alone. Reserve the higher end for flows where raw input changes code/data execution (injection, traversal, RCE shapes); use the lower end for render/redirect/header-style flows; use the lightest nudge when the value is partly constrained but the context-correct escape is still missing. Use your judgment to place a given flow; identical hunks should land at the same level.

NON-BLOCKING & NO INTENT. Describe the observable behaviour (raw input flowing into a sink) and show the shape of the fix for the single most consequential flow — never impute intent or character.

DEFER BOUNDARY. This practice judges ONLY a taint-to-sink injection flow; a generic missing bounds/null/format check with no dangerous sink belongs to validates-inputs-and-edge-cases-at-the-boundary, and a committed secret belongs to avoids-insecure-defaults-and-over-broad-permissions — defer to the sibling rather than double-flag.
practices.whyItMatters: Gluing raw user input straight into a query, a shell command, or a page is how injection bugs are born, the kind that leak a database or run an attacker's code. Parameterizing or escaping it first is what keeps that door shut.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: User-supplied values reach the database through bound parameters and the page through proper escaping, never concatenated into a SQL string or dropped into HTML as-is.

practices.name: Avoid insecure defaults and over-broad permissions
practices.whyItMatters: A wide-open CORS rule, a world-readable file, or a token with far more access than it needs sits quietly until someone exploits it. Starting locked down means you only open what you can justify.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A new endpoint or credential grants exactly the access it needs and no more, with scoped permissions and a specific allowed origin, instead of a permissive default dropped in to make things work.

practices.name: Change dependencies deliberately
practices.criteria:
## The standard
Judge whether this pull request edits its dependencies DELIBERATELY: when the manifest (where a human declares a dependency and its version) changes, the lockfile (the pinned resolution) moves with it, and a notable new dependency or version bump carries a one-line reason a reviewer can read. This is a supply-chain workflow habit (OWASP A06, reproducible builds) about how a dependency edit is justified and kept coherent — never a claim about the dependency's own correctness, its CVEs, or whether the chosen version is the "right" one.

## Good vs. bad (open examples — reason about the rest)
GOOD: a new direct runtime dependency arrives with a sentence on what it is for (in the manifest context, the PR title/body, or a commit subject) and the lockfile updates in the same change; a routine transitive resolution refresh that is internally consistent. BAD: a manifest adds or major-bumps a runtime dependency but the lockfile that exists for that ecosystem was left untouched (the build no longer resolves reproducibly from the manifest as written); a notable add lands as an unexplained pin with no quotable why; a version pin is loosened or dropped so a future build could silently resolve something else. Use your judgment as an experienced reviewer for manifests, lockfiles, and ecosystems not named here — the principle (coherence + a traceable reason for notable changes), not a fixed file list, decides. A patch/minor bump or a transitive-only delta needs no prose, and a large generated lockfile delta is normal EVIDENCE of coherence, not a problem.

## Presence, assessment, severity (as principles)
Emit a finding only when a real dependency surface (manifest or lockfile) is in the changed set and the deciding hunk is quotable; otherwise NOT_APPLICABLE. Coherent-and-explained is PRESENT, GOOD. Incoherence (manifest and lockfile out of step on something that ships) is the most serious case here; an unexplained-but-coherent notable pin is a milder, formative nudge; a loosened/dropped pin always deserves at least a gentle flag. Scale severity to how much reproducibility is actually at risk, and never exceed MAJOR on dependency deliberateness alone — this is a non-blocking habit to grow, not a merge gate. Describe the observable edit; never impute intent or character. Identical hunks must yield identical assessments.

## Grounding, boundaries, look-before-you-leap
Every claim cites observable evidence: quote the manifest line, the matching or missing lockfile span, and the explaining sentence/commit-subject verbatim. Before calling a manifest edit orphaned, confirm a lockfile for that ecosystem actually exists and was left out — if none exists in the repo, the manifest is coherent by absence. If the justification is not observable in the provided diff, title, body, or commit subjects, return NOT_APPLICABLE; never invent a file, a reason, a CVE, or a clean bill you did not verify. You see subjects, the file-spread table, and the quoted hunks, not per-commit patches — judge from what is quotable. Defer artifact-hygiene and review-size concerns about generated churn to its sibling practice; assess only deliberateness.
practices.whyItMatters: Every dependency you add is code you now trust and have to keep patched, and a lockfile that drifts from the manifest breaks reproducible builds. A one-line note on why it is there saves a future teammate from guessing whether it is safe to remove.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A new package arrives with a sentence on what it is for, the lockfile updated in the same commit as the manifest, and no leftover version churn in unrelated packages.

areas.name: Recording decisions and documenting changes
areas.description: Capture the why. Record the reasoning behind significant design decisions, and document changes to public APIs and shared behaviour, so the people who maintain or build on the code later can understand it.

practices.name: Record significant decisions and the reasoning
practices.criteria:
## The standard
This practice judges ONE thing: when this PR makes an architecturally SIGNIFICANT decision, is the WHY recorded somewhere a future maintainer can find it — a written rationale (constraint, trade-off, or alternative rejected) — rather than left for them to reverse-engineer from the code? Significance is the kind of choice a senior reviewer would expect a sentence about: a new dependency or framework, a schema/data-model change, a public-contract or wire-API change, a new cross-cutting mechanism the rest of the app plugs into (a shared aspect/interceptor/event bus, a base class many types extend), or an auth/security mechanism. These are illustrative, not a closed list — use your judgment about what is genuinely structural versus routine. Routine work (tests, formatting, docs, renames without contract change, a version bump of an already-present dependency, a tuned constant/threshold on an internal symbol) carries no architectural decision: emit NOT_APPLICABLE.

Two stages, in order: (1) decide whether the hand-written diff contains a significant decision; (2) ONLY if it does, check whether its rationale is recorded in the title, body, a commit subject, or a linked ADR/design-doc. A rationale clause counts whether it sits in prose or inside a bullet/checklist item ("chose X because Y", "an event queue so a slow consumer can't block checkout", "to avoid Z") — scan the whole body. Restating WHAT changed, an empty or template body, or a bare "added dependency X" does not record a why.

## Good vs bad
GOOD (PRESENT): a significant decision is present and its reasoning is written down, so the intent survives without re-reading the code. BAD: a significant decision ships with no recorded why anywhere in the body, the commit subjects, or a linked ADR.

## Severity philosophy
Non-blocking; never exceed MAJOR on rationale alone. A single contained unexplained decision (one new dependency, one isolated column, one app-internal class) is a light MINOR nudge. Reserve MAJOR for the genuinely consequential: an unexplained auth/security mechanism, a persistence/public-API/wire-contract change, a real cross-cutting mechanism others build on, or several significant decisions co-occurring unexplained. When unsure, prefer the lower severity.

## Grounding and stance
Ground every PRESENT/BAD finding in observable evidence: quote the exact added [L<n>] line(s) establishing significance, and for a GOOD finding also quote the verbatim rationale sentence/subject/reference. If the diff is absent/empty or metadata.enriched is false, there is nothing to scan — emit NOT_APPLICABLE; never treat a missing diff as routine or significant. Per-commit patches are not in your context — judge significance from the diff and subjects, never claim a per-commit file mapping you cannot see. Detect significance from hand-written hunks only; a dependency echoed solely in a lockfile (no manifest edit) is not an independent decision, but a hand-authored migration changeset is. Never invent a rationale the author did not write, never infer the why from the code itself, and never assert the dependency/schema/endpoint is wrong — judge only whether the WHY is recorded. Describe the observable behaviour; never impute intent or character.

## Defer boundary
The overall adequacy of the PR description (a clear title + a why for the change as a whole) belongs to describe-what-and-why; here judge only whether THAT significant decision's rationale is recorded, so the two never double-flag.

## Guidance shape
Feed-forward and decision-shaped: name the single most consequential undocumented decision, quote it, and show the shape of the fix (a one-line "## Why ..." to add, or a pointer to record an ADR) — never a finished rationale you authored.
practices.whyItMatters: When you pick one design over another, the reasoning is the first thing to fade. A maintainer who cannot find why you chose it will either undo a good decision or be too afraid to touch it, and both cost the team later.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A meaningful design choice arrives with its reasoning written down, in the description or a short architecture decision record (ADR), such as 'chose an event queue over direct calls so a slow consumer cannot block checkout', not just the code that does it.

practices.name: Document public API and behaviour changes
practices.criteria:
STANDARD. This practice judges one habit: when a change alters a PUBLIC/SHARED surface — something a SEPARATE consumer depends on and compiles or integrates against (a library/framework's exported symbols, a published package's API, a documented HTTP/RPC contract, a persisted or wire schema, an error/response shape callers branch on, a config default a consumer sets) — does that change carry, in the SAME diff or in metadata.body, documentation that tells consumers what moved? Good means the renamed parameter, new endpoint, changed response field, or breaking removal lands with its doc-comment, changelog line, or a body sentence naming the consumer-facing effect, so a downstream caller hears it from the change itself. Bad means a real exported-surface change ships with nothing a consumer could read to adapt.

GATE FIRST. A single application target with no library product has no public API: internal types, private signatures, local refactors, and app-only fields are NOT public surfaces — emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Use your judgment as an experienced reviewer to decide whether a changed symbol is actually exported and consumer-facing, rather than matching keywords; when genuinely unsure whether a symbol crosses a module/service boundary, treat it as internal and prefer NOT_APPLICABLE over a false BAD. Generated/vendored artifacts (codegen specs, *.gen.*, lockfiles, route trees, dist) count as neither a surface to flag nor as documentation — assess only the hand-written remainder.

ASSESSMENT & SEVERITY (principles, not a lookup table). Non-blocking and never exceeds MAJOR — this is a formative habit, not a merge gate. Scale severity to consumer impact: a breaking change (removed/renamed symbol, route, or field; a narrowed type; a changed default) shipping with no documentation anywhere is the most serious because callers break without a migration note (up to MAJOR); a new additive surface with no documentation is gentler because nothing breaks but consumers cannot discover it; documentation that exists yet never names the specific changed parameter/field is the gentlest nudge. When several surfaces change, grade by the least-documented one. Equivalent situations should land at equivalent severity.

GROUNDING. Cite the observable surface change and either quote the documenting text or, before concluding it is undocumented, look in all the places a consumer would: a doc-comment on or above the surface, a changelog/docs addition, and metadata.body. If the surface change is not observable in the provided diff and body, return NOT_APPLICABLE — never invent a symbol, an export marker, a changelog, or a clean bill you did not see. Describe only the documentation habit on the observable artifact; never assert compile/runtime correctness of the surface and never impute the author's intent or care.

DEFER. You judge only whether the public-surface change is documented for consumers — not the PR's general what/why narrative (describe-what-and-why) nor whether a design decision's rationale is recorded (records-significant-decisions-with-rationale). A thin body or unjustified choice is not a finding here; a finding requires an actual quotable public-surface change.
practices.whyItMatters: People outside your code depend on what you export. Change a public function or endpoint without a note and they find out at runtime, in their own project, with no warning, and they trust your library a little less each time.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A renamed parameter or a changed response shape lands with its reference docs and a changelog line updated in the same change, so the people building on your API hear about it from you, not from a broken build.

areas.name: Disciplined delivery and version control
areas.description: Keep your delivery clean. Make each commit one self-contained change, leave generated and build files out of the diff, and branch from the shared integration branch so your work stands on its own.

practices.name: Keep each commit to one logical change
practices.criteria:
## The standard
Judge one thing: whether the PR's commits are SPLIT so each one captures a single logical change, rather than one commit tangling unrelated kinds of work into a bundle a reviewer cannot follow, revert, or cherry-pick on its own. This is a workflow habit about how the history is partitioned — describe the observable split, never impute intent or character, and never frame it as a code-defect claim.

## What good vs bad looks like
GOOD: each commit reads as one cohesive logical unit — e.g. a fix and the test that covers it together, or a single change described in two clauses about the same step ("parse the header and validate it"). Multiple cohesive feature commits in one PR is fine here. BAD: a subject that plainly conjoins distinct concerns (e.g. a behavioural fix bundled with an unrelated reformat/lint sweep, a rename-sweep, a merge-conflict resolution, or a dependency bump), or a series that obviously braids process/chore work through behavioural work. Use your judgment as an experienced reviewer about what counts as "unrelated" — no fixed word list; a conjunction joining clauses about the SAME change is not a tangle.

## Presence / assessment / severity
PRESENT, GOOD when the commits read as cohesive logical units. assessment=BAD when the split itself is wrong. Severity is non-blocking and never exceeds MAJOR on commit atomicity alone: lean MINOR when a single commit bundles unrelated work (splitting it would isolate each concern), and MAJOR only when the history broadly tangles so a reviewer cannot reason about or revert one concern at a time. This is a formative habit to grow, not a merge gate.

## Grounding and honest context boundaries
Your evidence is the commit SUBJECT lines plus the per-file changed-line spread; per-commit patches are NOT in your context, so you cannot see which files belong to which commit — judge conservatively from subjects and overall spread, and never claim a per-commit file mapping you cannot observe. Quote the deciding subject(s) for any BAD finding. The commit set is unordered and dateless — never reason about sequence or "which came first". Every claim must cite an observable subject; if you cannot quote a subject that conjoins or braids unrelated concerns, treat the history as cohesive (PRESENT, GOOD) — never invent a tangle the subjects do not state. Emit NOT_APPLICABLE when the split cannot be assessed: no commit subjects observable, only a single non-merge commit (no split to judge), or every entry is an automatic merge commit. Do NOT attach a file/diff-line location to this finding — its evidence is the subject text, not a diff line.

## Defer boundary
Defer overall PR size and file spread to scope-one-reviewable-change, and subject wording/legibility to commit-subjects-explain-each-change — a vague-but-cohesive subject, or a large-but-cohesive change, is a strength here, not a tangle.
practices.whyItMatters: A commit that mixes an unrelated refactor in with a bug fix cannot be cleanly reverted or cherry-picked, so you are stuck taking the bad with the good. One logical change per commit keeps your history a tool you can act on instead of a tangle.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: Separate commits for separate ideas, such as 'Fix off-by-one in pagination' in one and 'Rename helper for clarity' in another, so each can be reviewed, reverted, or backported on its own.

practices.name: Keep generated and build files out of the diff
practices.criteria:
## The standard
This practice judges ONE thing: does the change keep machine-emitted output and committed cruft OUT of the diff, so a reviewer reads only what a human hand-wrote? Treat it as a repository-hygiene habit about *what gets committed* — never as a code-defect claim about the file's contents.

Judge from the file PATHS in the diff (the per-file table and the per-file headers). You MAY open the patch to confirm a flagged path is machine-emitted when the path alone is ambiguous — e.g. a "DO NOT EDIT"/"@generated"/"auto-generated" banner, or a minified single-line bundle — but the evidence is the path plus that confirming line, not a line-by-line review of the file's contents.

## What good vs bad looks like
GOOD: every changed path is something a person authored or intended — source, tests, docs, config you wrote, or a lockfile that moves in step with its manifest in the same diff. Use your judgment as an experienced reviewer: a build-output directory, an editor/OS cruft file, a coverage dump, a vendored/cached blob, or a tracked generated file someone hand-edited instead of regenerating are all signs that machine noise is being reviewed as if it were source. These are open examples, not a closed list — recognise the underlying shape (a location or banner that says "a tool wrote this") across any stack or language.

A repo may DELIBERATELY version-control some generated sources (a committed OpenAPI spec, a generated client, an ERD, a routeTree). When such a tracked artifact moves IN STEP with the hand-written change that drives its regeneration, that is intended, regeneration-consistent movement — do not flag it. Flag only output that is not meant to be tracked, or a tracked generated file left hand-edited/stale relative to its source.

## Presence, assessment, severity (as principles)
PRESENT when the diff carries quotable file paths whose committed-hygiene you can read; otherwise NOT_APPLICABLE. Also return NOT_APPLICABLE when machine-emitted paths are essentially the whole diff — a pure regeneration/build commit has no hand-authored remainder whose hygiene this practice can meaningfully judge; do not blanket-grade an intentional regeneration as BAD.

GOOD when no changed path is machine-emitted output or cruft. BAD when at least one is. Severity is non-blocking and never exceeds MAJOR on this practice alone: a lone stray artifact is minor; a committed build-output directory, several leaked artifacts, or a hand-edited generated file that should have been regenerated weighs toward MAJOR. Use reviewer judgment on where in that band a case sits; identical situations should land identically.

## Grounding and stance
Every BAD finding must quote each offending path verbatim and, for a generated-file flag, quote the confirming banner or name the tracked artifact. If you cannot quote an offending path, there is nothing to flag — do not invent one, and never assert compile/build/runtime behaviour from a path. Describe the observable fact (this path is machine-emitted) and the next step (gitignore it and drop it, or regenerate from source); never impute intent or character. This is a habit to grow, not a merge gate.

## Defer boundary
Whether a dependency change was deliberate or justified belongs to the sibling changes-dependencies-deliberately, not here — a lockfile moving in step with its manifest is its concern, not yours; you flag a lockfile only when it is hand-edited out of step with any manifest in the diff.
practices.whyItMatters: Committing a build output or an editor config bloats the diff with machine noise nobody reviews, and it creates merge conflicts in files no human wrote. Leaving them out gives the reviewer a change they can actually read.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: The diff holds only the source you hand-wrote, with build outputs, logs, and editor files kept out through .gitignore, and no generated bundle riding along unnoticed.

practices.name: Branch from the shared integration branch
practices.criteria:
DEFECT-DETECTOR DISCIPLINE (overrides any "assessment=GOOD" rule below): this practice catches one specific defect — a branch cut off an in-flight feature branch instead of the team's shared integration branch. A clean cut is the baseline, not a craft act to endorse; never emit (PRESENT, GOOD), because that would assert an unverifiable clean bill of health. Only two outcomes exist: (PRESENT, BAD) with a quotable off-integration signal, or NOT_APPLICABLE.

STANDARD. Each change should branch from the shared line everyone merges into — typically main, but use your judgment about what the team's integration branch is here (it might be named develop, trunk, release/*, or a stack-specific convention). Cutting instead from someone else's unfinished feature branch drags that branch's unmerged commits into yours, tangling the two diffs and coupling your merge to theirs landing first. You are pointing at a version-control delivery habit — where the work was cut from — never a code-defect claim and never an assertion that the history is broken.

WHAT IT LOOKS LIKE. BAD: the target branch is plainly not the integration line (e.g. it points at another open feature branch), or the git history shows this branch carries a long run of commits from several distinct authors that are not on the integration branch — a tell it was started off an in-flight branch rather than freshly cut. Treat the commit-spread pattern as a soft signal, not proof: a long-lived solo branch that merged the integration line back in can legitimately accrue commits and authors. Weigh it as an experienced reviewer would.

EVIDENCE & GROUNDING. Judge from metadata.json (source_branch, target_branch, commit list) and the mounted clone at inputs/sources/scm/repo (inspect the real git history — the ahead-range versus the integration branch and the authors across it). You do NOT see the per-commit diffs or subjects; reason only about branch facts you can actually read, and quote them (name the target branch; quote whatever history facts you inspected). If the branch names and history carry no clear off-integration signal — or are absent or ambiguous — emit NOT_APPLICABLE. Never invent an ahead-range, an author, a conflict, or a clean bill you did not verify; abstain rather than infer from silence.

SEVERITY. Non-blocking and formative: a low-cost habit nudge, never a merge gate. Cap at MINOR on this signal alone. Identical branch facts should yield the same outcome. Describe the observable branching behaviour and suggest cutting the next branch from the up-to-date integration line; never impute intent or character.

DEFER. You judge only where the branch was cut from — not commit cohesion, diff scope, or merge mechanics, which sibling practices own.
practices.whyItMatters: Cutting your branch off a half-finished feature branch drags all of its unmerged work into yours, tangling the two and making your diff impossible to review on its own. Branching from the shared integration branch, the one everyone merges into, often main, keeps your work independent.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A branch cut fresh from the integration branch that carries only your own commits, so the diff shows your change alone and not a pile of someone else's in-flight work.

areas.name: Tracking and planning the work
areas.description: Plan and track the work so a maintainer can route it and watch it progress. Split genuinely large issues into trackable sub-tasks, and triage each one with the labels, assignee, or milestone that tells the team where it belongs.

practices.name: Break large work into trackable subtasks
practices.criteria:
Judge ONE thing: when an issue describes genuinely LARGE or multi-part work, is that work broken into explicit, trackable sub-units a maintainer can watch reach done — rather than left as a single monolithic ask? Trackable means the parts are individually checkable or addressable: a task list of checkboxes that name work to do, a tracked sub-issue tree, or enumerated child/tracking issues. Reason like a senior reviewer scanning a backlog: a big ask written as one undivided block is either 0% or 100% done with no visible progress; a decomposed one lets pieces be picked up in parallel and tracked to done.

GOOD looks like: a large issue split into named sub-steps (e.g. "design the schema", "add the endpoint", "wire up the UI") as a checklist, or as linked child issues the body frames as parts. BAD looks like: a clearly large, multi-deliverable ask carrying no breakdown of any kind — e.g. an epic/tracking-style issue with nothing tracked under it, or a body that enumerates several independent deliverables as prose with no way to track each.

Use your judgment on what counts as LARGE. The signal is two or more SEPARABLE deliverables that could ship or be assigned independently, or an explicit epic/initiative/meta/tracking framing the author themselves chose. Sequential steps of ONE cohesive flow described as a journey are a single deliverable, not several parts — when genuinely unsure whether spans are one journey or several deliverables, prefer NOT_APPLICABLE. A focused single-concern issue (one feature, one bug) is not large: it neither needs nor is penalised for lacking a checklist.

Presence/assessment/severity as principles, not a lookup table: emit PRESENT, GOOD when a breakdown is quotable OR the work is not large enough to need one. Emit assessment=BAD only when the work is clearly large AND no trackable breakdown exists on any surface. Scale severity to the gap, never above MAJOR (this is a non-blocking workflow habit, not a merge gate): heaviest when an explicitly epic/tracking-framed issue has nothing to track; lighter when only a couple of parts go untracked. Identical evidence should yield the same call.

GROUNDING: every "large" claim and every "no breakdown" claim must quote the exact span — a label name, body text, the sub_issues rollup figure, or a comment. Before concluding a breakdown is absent, actually scan ALL of: body, the sub_issues_total/sub_issues_completed rollup, and comments — look before you leap. An element you cannot quote is counted absent; never invent largeness from title length or tone, and never fabricate a breakdown you did not see. Your context is the ISSUE only — title, body, labels, the metadata rollup, and comments; there is NO diff or code, so never assert technical feasibility, hour-estimates, or design correctness, and never grade the reporter as a person. Describe observable artifact state only.

DEFER boundary: this practice asks only whether large work is DECOMPOSED. Whether the work SHOULD have been one concern, and whether acceptance-criteria / Definition-of-Done conditions are present, are owned by sibling practices — treat a multi-part ask as legitimately large work needing a breakdown, and do not re-flag a verification checklist as if it were a work-breakdown unless it also names concrete implementation sub-steps.

NOT_APPLICABLE when the issue is a pure question, support request, or open discussion with no concrete deliverable; when the body is empty, title-only, or an unmodified template (nothing quotable); or when it is a small single-concern deliverable so the breakdown question does not arise.

Guidance is feed-forward ("where to next?"): when large work lacks a breakdown, name the parts you quoted and show only the artifact to add — a "## Tasks" checklist skeleton or a "Tracked in: #…" line — never author the sub-tasks for the reporter.
practices.whyItMatters: A big ask written as one wall of text is either 0% or 100% done, with no way to see progress or split the load. Breaking it into checkable steps turns a daunting job into pieces people can pick up and finish one at a time.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: A genuinely large issue split into a task list or linked sub-issues, such as 'design the schema', 'add the endpoint', and 'wire up the UI', so progress is visible and pieces can be worked in parallel.

practices.name: Triage the issue with labels and ownership
practices.criteria:
## The standard
Judge whether this issue carries enough categorizing metadata that a maintainer scanning the board can ROUTE and PLAN it without opening it. A well-triaged issue is typed, placed in an area/component, and/or given a priority signal, ideally with an owner and a milestone — so it can be filtered, assigned, and scheduled at a glance. You assess PRESENCE of routing metadata, not its accuracy: never assert that an applied label is the correct one, never claim an assignee is the right owner, never judge whether the work is worth doing.

## What good vs bad looks like
GOOD: at least one label that meaningfully helps route or plan — a TYPE (e.g. bug, feature, chore), an AREA/COMPONENT (e.g. backend, ui, a module/team name), or a PRIORITY/SEVERITY/EFFORT signal (e.g. p1, critical, size). Stay vocabulary-agnostic: do not require any house taxonomy, prefix, namespace, or color — use your judgment about whether a given label name plausibly aids routing. Purely social/process-noise labels (e.g. "good first issue", "help wanted", "duplicate", "wontfix", "question") do not, on their own, make an issue routable. An owner or a milestone is a weaker routing signal than a categorizing label.
BAD: a substantive issue carries no signal a maintainer could filter or plan on.

## Presence / assessment / severity
This is a lightweight routing-hygiene nudge, non-blocking, never a merge gate; it never exceeds MINOR on triage metadata alone. As a principle of precedence: a categorizing label is the strongest signal; an owner or milestone is weaker; nothing is unroutable. So — PRESENT/GOOD when a categorizing label is present (regardless of owner/milestone); a gentle INFO when there is no categorizing label but the issue is at least owned or scheduled; MINOR when a substantive issue has none of these. Identical metadata yields identical assessment.

NOT_APPLICABLE when there is no unit of work to route (a pure question, support request, or open discussion has nothing to land on a board — never demand routing labels of it), OR when the metadata needed to judge presence is genuinely not in your context. Distinguish absent metadata from empty metadata: if labels/assignees/milestone are simply not observable, abstain — never read missing fields as an untriaged set. An empty body on a substantive issue with no routing signal is BAD, not abstention.

## Grounding, boundaries, stance
Ground every finding in observable metadata: quote the exact label name(s) you relied on, and state whether an owner and milestone are set. If the relevant fact is not observable, return NOT_APPLICABLE — never invent a label, owner, milestone, or clean bill you did not verify. Describe only the observable triage state; never impute intent or character.
Defer prose quality (problem statement, scope, definition of done) to the sibling G3 issue practices — a weak problem statement with a categorizing label present is still GOOD here; do not double-flag. Guidance is feed-forward: name the single most useful missing routing signal and the shape to add (e.g. "add a type or area label so this can be filtered on the board"), without dictating which specific label is correct or who should own it.
practices.whyItMatters: An untriaged issue is invisible at planning: no one knows whose area it is, how urgent it is, or which release it belongs to, so it sinks to the bottom of the backlog. A few labels make it routable at a glance.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: An issue tagged with its type and area, assigned to an owner, and placed in a milestone, so a maintainer scanning the board can route and plan it without opening it.

practices.name: Confirm the outcome before closing the issue
practices.criteria:
## The standard
When an issue is CLOSED as done, judge whether its own stated definition-of-done was actually confirmed before the close — every checkable acceptance item ticked and every tracked sub-issue finished — versus the issue being closed with a done-condition still visibly open. Frame this strictly as a lifecycle habit ("confirm the outcome before you close"), NEVER as a claim that the work itself is wrong or incomplete: an empty checkbox may simply never have been ticked while the work was genuinely done, or the remainder may be tracked elsewhere.

You read only the issue text in your context — title, body, labels, state, state_reason, the sub-issue rollup (completed of total), closed_at, and comment bodies. There is no diff or code here. Never assert compile/runtime/correctness behaviour, and never read a missing or absent rollup as zero.

## What good vs needs-work looks like
GOOD: closed as done with every checkable item ticked and every tracked sub-issue completed — "closed" honestly means done. NEEDS-WORK: closed as done while a stated done-item is still open, e.g. an unchecked acceptance/Definition-of-Done line, or a sub-issue rollup short of complete, with nothing at close naming where that remainder went.

## Presence, assessment, severity (principles)
PRESENT only when the issue is genuinely CLOSED *as done* AND it actually stated a checkable outcome you can quote (a task-list, an acceptance/DoD block, a Given-When-Then, or a non-zero sub-issue rollup). If the issue isn't closed, was closed as not-planned / wontfix / duplicate / superseded / deferred (an unmet checklist is the *expected* state for deliberately-abandoned work), or stated no checkable outcome at all, return NOT_APPLICABLE and stay silent. Read the disposition from the observable fact — prefer an explicit state_reason, else an explicit closing-comment/body signal; a bare close with no not-planned signal defaults to done. Do not invent a state_reason the artifact never carried.

Assessment is GOOD when the stated outcome was confirmed, BAD when a stated done-item was left open at close. Severity is a judgment, not a lookup: an unconfirmed *acceptance/DoD* condition is the heavier lapse; an open *sub-issue rollup* alone is lighter. Crucially, LOOK BEFORE YOU CONCLUDE — scan every closing comment and the body for a disposition that explains the remainder (e.g. "deferred to #123", "covered by !45", "done, forgot to tick"). If the disposition was named at close, the habit was largely honoured, so keep severity low; reserve the higher band for an open done-item with *nothing anywhere* accounting for it. This practice is non-blocking and never exceeds MAJOR on outcome-confirmation alone; identical inputs yield identical severity.

## Grounding and stance
Every claim cites an exact quoted span — the unchecked line, the "N of M" rollup, the state/state_reason value. A BAD finding must quote the deciding span; an abstention names what you scanned (body, comments, rollup, state). If the deciding fact isn't observable, return NOT_APPLICABLE — never fabricate a checklist, a clean bill, or a disposition.

Describe only observable state ("closed as done with '<quoted line>' still unchecked"), never a charge against the closer ("you left it unfinished"). The feed-forward lesson is the habit: before closing, tick the items, close the child issues, or leave a comment naming what's deferred and filing a follow-up.

## Defer boundary
Whether a *new/open* issue defines a checkable outcome belongs to the sibling issue-has-checkable-outcome; this practice owns only the single at-close question of whether a stated outcome was confirmed.
practices.whyItMatters: Closing an issue tells the whole team it is handled. Closing it with a checklist item still unticked or sub-issues still open drops that work quietly, and someone hits the gap later expecting it to be done.
practices.whatGoodLooksLike: Before closing, every acceptance item is checked and every tracked sub-issue is finished, or a comment says plainly what is being dropped and why, so 'closed' honestly means done.
